planting.note: Direct sow in fall to allow seeds to naturally stratify over winter before spring germination.

directions: Narrowleaf Barbara's Buttons is a low-growing native of longleaf pine savannas and roadsides along the southeastern coastal plain, prized for its soft pink-purple pincushion flower heads that appear from late spring through summer. Sow seeds directly in fall into well-drained, sandy or loamy soil where they will cold-stratify naturally and germinate the following spring. This plant tolerates both full sun and partial shade but performs best with some afternoon protection in the hotter parts of its range. Once established, it spreads slowly into attractive clumps and requires little maintenance beyond removing spent flower heads if self-seeding is not desired.

planting.note: Sow in fall into sandy, well-drained soil to allow natural cold stratification before spring germination.

directions: Spoonleaf Barbara's Buttons is a compact southeastern native wildflower adapted to the sandy, nutrient-poor soils of longleaf pine uplands, where it produces distinctive spoon-shaped basal leaves and delicate white to pale pink pincushion blooms in spring. Direct sow seeds in fall into well-drained, acidic sandy soil with good sun exposure, spacing plants at least 9 inches apart to accommodate their slow-spreading habit. This drought-tolerant perennial is well-suited to rock gardens, native plant borders, and open pine understory plantings where many other flowers struggle. It thrives with minimal fertilization, as overly rich soils can reduce flowering and encourage lush but bloom-sparse foliage.

planting.note: Fall direct sowing mimics natural seed dispersal and provides the cold stratification needed for reliable spring germination.

directions: False Boneset is a tough, drought-tolerant prairie and meadow perennial native to central and eastern North America, bearing clusters of creamy white flower heads from late summer into fall when many other wildflowers have finished blooming. It is especially valued as a critical late-season nectar source for monarch butterflies and native bees fueling up before migration or overwintering. Sow seeds directly in fall into dry, well-drained soils in full sun; this species tolerates poor, rocky, or clay soils and actually performs best without supplemental fertilization. Once established, False Boneset is long-lived, self-seeds moderately, and integrates well into naturalistic prairie plantings and pollinator gardens.

planting.note: Sow in fall to allow seeds to naturally stratify; germination is often slow and benefits from the full cold period.

directions: Cusp Blazing Star is a drought-tolerant native of Texas, Oklahoma, and New Mexico prairies and cedar brakes, producing slender stems densely clad with narrow, pointed leaves topped by vivid lavender-purple flower spikes in late summer. It is an excellent choice for hot, dry south-central gardens where many other Liatris species struggle, offering outstanding ornamental value with almost no supplemental irrigation once established. Direct sow seeds in fall into lean, well-drained soil in full sun, as this species does not tolerate wet or heavy clay conditions. Plants bloom most prolifically when grown in poor to moderately fertile soil and left undisturbed to develop their deep corms over several seasons.

planting.note: Direct sow at or just after last frost once soil has warmed; plants spread by rhizomes and will naturalize readily.

directions: Long-leaf Ground Cherry is a hardy native husk tomato found across central and eastern North America in prairies, disturbed areas, and open woodlands, producing small yellow fruits enclosed in papery husks similar to a miniature tomatillo that are edible fresh or cooked. Direct sow seeds after the last frost into well-drained soil in full sun, as this perennial spreads vigorously from spreading rhizomes and will naturalize in favorable conditions. Plants are extremely drought-tolerant once established and require little care beyond occasional thinning to prevent them from crowding out neighboring plants. Harvest fruits when the husk has turned tan and dried, and the berry inside has dropped naturally, as this indicates peak ripeness and sweetness.
